key_learnings: Supplements should complement, not replace, a nutrient-dense whole-food diet. Evidence quality varies enormously across supplements — very few have robust RCT support for clinical outcomes. Bioavailability of supplement forms differs significantly (e.g., magnesium glycinate vs. oxide; methylcobalamin vs. cyanocobalamin for B12).
research_findings: Vitamin D3 + K2 co-supplementation is more effective for bone health than D3 alone by ensuring calcium is directed to bones rather than arteries. Magnesium deficiency affects up to 50% of adults and is underdiagnosed by standard serum testing (which reflects only 1% of total body magnesium). Omega-3 fish oil supplementation (2–4g EPA+DHA/day) reduces triglycerides by 20–30% and is FDA-approved for hypertriglyceridemia.
current_focus_areas: Building an evidence-tiered supplement recommendation engine (Tier 1: strong RCT evidence; Tier 2: observational/mechanistic; Tier 3: emerging/preliminary); developing contraindication and drug-nutrient interaction screening; integrating with Bot 5 (Micronutrient Deficiency Detection) to prioritize supplement recommendations by detected deficiencies.
